original: You pull the bottle out but remember that you need to have horns in order use it!\n\n
You pull the bottle out and turn it over in your hands, trying to decide what color you’d like.\n\n
e.capitalA+e.short+" uses the Hornucopia but to no effect.
You put the bottle back into your inventory.\n\n
You flick the color picker to the ‘natural’ option, swirling the brush around in the bottle of varnish. It starts to take on a thinner consistency, the color leaving it.
You flick the color picker to your chosen color, swirling the brush around in the bottle of varnish until the color takes.
\n\nYou take out your codex, turning on the camera application and making sure to angle it so you can see your "+i+" properly - you don’t want to miss a spot!
\n\nPainting the varnish on takes quite a while with how small the brush is, but you’re eventually done. You sit back, letting it do it’s work.

\n\nAs you watch, the "+t+" toned varnish you applied seems to be soaking into your "+i+", the color no longer just surface deep. Once you’re sure that you’re not going to rub anything off, you reach your hand up to touch your "+i+".

\n\n"+(1===n?"It feels":"They feel")+" the same as ever - no feeling of sticky or set varnish left on "+(1===n?"it":"them")+", and you’re satisfied that you now have "+(1===n?l.m.indefiniteArticle(t):t)+" "+GLOBAL.TYPE_NAMES[e.hornType].toLowerCase()+" "+i+"!

\n\nAs you watch, your "+i+" seem"+(1===n?"s":"")+" to visibly harden, becoming slightly more angular in appearance. The "+t+" toned varnish you applied seems to be soaking into your "+i+" too, the color no longer just surface deep. Once you’re sure that you’re not going to rub anything off, you reach your hand up to touch your "+i+".

\n\nYou were expecting a simple color change, but feeling this, it’s almost like your "+i+" have been coated, or maybe even replaced, with an actual metal! Looking at your codex, "+(1===n?"it’s":"they’re")+" even subtly reflective.
\n\nA check of your codex confirms that it’s not quite the same as real metal, though, so you won’t have to worry about getting electrocuted any more than usual.

\n\nAs you watch, your "+i+" seem"+(1===n?"s":"")+" to visibly harden, becoming slightly more smooth in appearance. The "+t+" toned varnish you applied seems to be soaking into your "+i+" too, the color no longer just surface deep. The change is more pronounced than just the color though, your "+i+" seem"+(1===n?"s":"")+" to be changing in density, even becoming slightly translucent. You were expecting just a color change, but once the transformation stops, a quick touch of your "+i+" confirms that it really is like your "+i+" "+(1===n?"has":"have")+" been turned completely into gemstone!
\n\nA check of your codex confirms that it’s not quite the same as real gemstone, though, so you won’t have to worry about someone trying to saw "+(1===n?"it":"them")+" off for profit.

\n\nAs you watch, the unnatural coloration of your "+i+" begins to fade, leaving behind traditional "+GLOBAL.TYPE_NAMES[e.hornType].toLowerCase()+" "+i+". After a while, the varnish has completely wicked away, and your "+i+" "+(1===n?"is":"are")+" now "+(1===n?"its":"their")+" natural color and appearance!
